title: XXXI
html:
Johannesburg,
March 7th.

Pagett has arrived. He is in a blue funk of course. Suggested at once that we should go off to Pretoria. Then, when I had told him kindly but firmly that we were going to remain here, he went to the other extreme, wished he had his rifle here, and began bucking about some bridge he guarded during the Great War. A railway bridge at Little Puddecombe junction, or something of that sort.

I soon cut that short by telling him to unpack the big typewriter. I thought that that would keep him employed for some time, because the typewriter was sure to have gone wrongÔªø‚Äîit always doesÔªø‚Äîand he would have to take it somewhere to be mended. But I had forgotten Pagett‚Äôs powers of being in the right.

‚ÄúI‚Äôve already unpacked all the cases, Sir Eustace. The typewriter is in perfect condition.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat do you meanÔªø‚Äîall the cases?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe two small cases as well.‚Äù

‚ÄúI wish you wouldn‚Äôt be so officious, Pagett. Those small cases were no business of yours. They belong to Mrs.¬ÝBlair.‚Äù

Pagett looked crestfallen. He hates to make a mistake.

‚ÄúSo you can just pack them up again neatly,‚Äù I continued. ‚ÄúAfter that you can go out and look around you. Jo‚Äôburg will probably be a heap of smoking ruins by tomorrow, so it may be your last chance.‚Äù

I thought that that would get rid of him successfully for the morning, at any rate.

‚ÄúThere is something I want to say to you when you have the leisure, Sir Eustace.‚Äù

‚ÄúI haven‚Äôt got it now,‚Äù I said hastily. ‚ÄúAt this minute I have absolutely no leisure whatsoever.‚Äù

Pagett retired.

‚ÄúBy the way,‚Äù I called after him, ‚Äúwhat was there in those cases of Mrs.¬ÝBlair‚Äôs?‚Äù

‚ÄúSome fur rugs, and a couple of furÔªø‚Äîhats, I think.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs right,‚Äù I assented. ‚ÄúShe bought them on the train. They are hatsÔªø‚Äîof a kindÔªø‚Äîthough I hardly wonder at your not recognizing them. I dare say she‚Äôs going to wear one of them at Ascot. What else was there?‚Äù

‚ÄúSome rolls of films and some basketsÔªø‚Äîa lot of basketsÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúThere would be,‚Äù I assured him. ‚ÄúMrs.¬ÝBlair is the kind of woman who never buys less than a dozen or so of anything.‚Äù

‚ÄúI think that‚Äôs all, Sir Eustace, except some miscellaneous odds and ends, a motor-veil and some odd glovesÔªø‚Äîthat sort of thing.‚Äù

‚ÄúIf you hadn‚Äôt been a born idiot, Pagett, you would have seen from the start that those couldn‚Äôt possibly be my belongings.‚Äù

‚ÄúI thought some of them might belong to Miss Pettigrew.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh, that reminds meÔªø‚Äîwhat do you mean by picking me out such a doubtful character as a secretary?‚Äù

And I told him about the searching cross-examination I had been put through. Immediately I was sorry, I saw a glint in his eye that I knew only too well. I changed the conversation hurriedly. But it was too late. Pagett was on the warpath.

He next proceeded to bore me with a long pointless story about the Kilmorden. It was about a roll of films and a wager. The roll of films being thrown through a porthole in the middle of the night by some steward who ought to have known better. I hate horseplay. I told Pagett so, and he began to tell me the story all over again. He tells a story extremely badly, anyway. It was a long time before I could make head or tail of this one.

I did not see him again until lunchtime. Then he came in brimming over with excitement, like a bloodhound on the scent. I never have cared for bloodhounds. The upshot of it all was that he had seen Rayburn.

‚ÄúWhat?‚Äù I cried, startled.

Yes, he had caught sight of someone whom he was sure was Rayburn crossing the street. Pagett had followed him.

‚ÄúAnd who do you think I saw him stop and speak to? Miss Pettigrew!‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, Sir Eustace. And that‚Äôs not all. I‚Äôve been making inquiries about herÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúWait a bit. What happened to Rayburn?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe and Miss Pettigrew went into that corner curio shopÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

I uttered an involuntary exclamation. Pagett stopped inquiringly.

‚ÄúNothing,‚Äù I said. ‚ÄúGo on.‚Äù

‚ÄúI waited outside for agesÔªø‚Äîbut they didn‚Äôt come out. At last I went in. Sir Eustace, there was no one in the shop! There must be another way out.‚Äù

I stared at him.

‚ÄúAs I was saying, I came back to the hotel and made some inquiries about Miss Pettigrew.‚Äù Pagett lowered his voice and breathed hard as he always does when he wants to be confidential. ‚ÄúSir Eustace, a man was seen coming out of her room last night.‚Äù

I raised my eyebrows.

‚ÄúAnd I always regarded her as a lady of such eminent respectability,‚Äù I murmured.

Pagett went on without heeding.

‚ÄúI went straight up and searched her room. What do you think I found?‚Äù

I shook my head.

‚ÄúThis!‚Äù

Pagett held up a safety razor and a stick of shaving soap.

‚ÄúWhat should a woman want with these?‚Äù

I don‚Äôt suppose Pagett ever reads the advertisements in the high-class ladies‚Äô papers. I do. Whilst not proposing to argue with him on the subject, I refused to accept the presence of the razor as proof positive of Miss Pettigrew‚Äôs sex. Pagett is so hopelessly behind the times. I should not have been at all surprised if he had produced a cigarette case to support his theory. However, even Pagett has his limits.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre not convinced, Sir Eustace. What do you say to this?‚Äù

I inspected the article which he dangled aloft triumphantly.

‚ÄúIt looks like hair,‚Äù I remarked distastefully.

‚ÄúIt is hair. I think it‚Äôs what they call a toupee.‚Äù

‚ÄúIndeed,‚Äù I commented.

‚ÄúNow are you convinced that that Pettigrew woman is a man in disguise?‚Äù

‚ÄúReally, my dear Pagett, I think I am. I might have known it by her feet.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen that‚Äôs that. And now, Sir Eustace, I want to speak to you about my private affairs. I cannot doubt, from your hints and your continual allusions to the time I was in Florence, that you have found me out.‚Äù

At last the mystery of what Pagett did in Florence is going to be revealed!

‚ÄúMake a clean breast of it, my dear fellow,‚Äù I said kindly. ‚ÄúMuch the best way.‚Äù

‚ÄúThank you, Sir Eustace.‚Äù

‚ÄúIs it her husband? Annoying fellows, husbands. Always turning up when they‚Äôre least expected.‚Äù

‚ÄúI fail to follow you, Sir Eustace. Whose husband?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe lady‚Äôs husband.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat lady?‚Äù

‚ÄúGod bless my soul, Pagett, the lady you met in Florence. There must have been a lady. Don‚Äôt tell me that you merely robbed a church or stabbed an Italian in the back because you didn‚Äôt like his face.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am quite at a loss to understand you, Sir Eustace. I suppose you are joking.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am an amusing fellow sometimes, when I take the trouble, but I can assure you that I am not trying to be funny this minute.‚Äù

‚ÄúI hoped that as I was a good way off you had not recognized me, Sir Eustace.‚Äù

‚ÄúRecognized you where?‚Äù

‚ÄúAt Marlow, Sir Eustace?‚Äù

‚ÄúAt Marlow? What the devil were you doing at Marlow?‚Äù

‚ÄúI thought you understood thatÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm beginning to understand less and less. Go back to the beginning of the story and start again. You went to FlorenceÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúThen you don‚Äôt know after allÔªø‚Äîand you didn‚Äôt recognize me!‚Äù

‚ÄúAs far as I can judge, you seem to have given yourself away needlesslyÔªø‚Äîmade a coward of by your conscience. But I shall be able to tell better when I‚Äôve heard the whole story. Now, then, take a deep breath and start again. You went to FlorenceÔªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúBut I didn‚Äôt go to Florence. That is just it.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, where did you go, then?‚Äù

‚ÄúI went homeÔªø‚Äîto Marlow.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat the devil did you want to go to Marlow for?‚Äù

‚ÄúI wanted to see my wife. She was in delicate health and expectingÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúYour wife? But I didn‚Äôt know you were married?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, Sir Eustace, that is just what I am telling you. I deceived you in this matter.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow long have you been married?‚Äù

‚ÄúJust over eight years. I had been married just six months when I became your secretary. I did not want to lose the post. A resident secretary is not supposed to have a wife, so I suppressed the fact.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou take my breath away,‚Äù I remarked. ‚ÄúWhere has she been all these years?‚Äù

‚ÄúWe have had a small bungalow on the river at Marlow, quite close to the Mill House, for over five years.‚Äù

‚ÄúGod bless my soul,‚Äù I muttered. ‚ÄúAny children?‚Äù

‚ÄúFour children, Sir Eustace.‚Äù

I gazed at him in a kind of stupor. I might have known, all along, that a man like Pagett couldn‚Äôt have a guilty secret. The respectability of Pagett has always been my bane. That‚Äôs just the kind of secret he would haveÔªø‚Äîa wife and four children.

‚ÄúHave you told this to anyone else?‚Äù I demanded at last, when I had gazed at him in fascinated interest for quite a long while.

‚ÄúOnly Miss Beddingfeld. She came to the station at Kimberley.‚Äù

I continued to stare at him. He fidgeted under my glance.

‚ÄúI hope, Sir Eustace, that you are not seriously annoyed?‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear fellow,‚Äù I said, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt mind telling you here and now that you‚Äôve blinking well torn it!‚Äù

I went out seriously ruffled. As I passed the corner curio shop, I was assailed by a sudden irresistible temptation and went in. The proprietor came forward obsequiously, rubbing his hands.

‚ÄúCan I show you something? Furs, curios?‚Äù

‚ÄúI want something quite out of the ordinary,‚Äù I said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs for a special occasion. Will you show me what you‚Äôve got?‚Äù

‚ÄúPerhaps you will come into my back room? We have many specialties there?‚Äù

That is where I made a mistake. And I thought I was going to be so clever. I followed him through the swinging portieres.
